title: Needle-felted pumpkin set
subtitle: Three pumpkins in orange, deep orange, and pale cream, 6-10 cm tall, with wrapped-and-pinched vertical ribs
excerpt: Three pumpkins needle-felted in orange, deep orange, and pale cream merino, ranging from 6 to 10 cm tall. Each pumpkin has a short brown felted stem and vertical ribs created by wrapping thread around the body and stabbing along each wrapped line to pinch it in. The ribbing is added after the base ball is fully compacted.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from contemporary needle-felting practice. The wrap-and-pinch ribbing technique is the standard pumpkin form taught across modern felting workshops; the method is synthesised from observation rather than a single named source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A needle-felting technique for creating raised ridges on a round form. Strong thread is wrapped around the outside of a compacted ball at evenly spaced intervals, then a felting needle is stabbed along each wrapped line to push the fibre inward at that point, creating a pinched rib. The thread is cut away once all ribs are defined, leaving permanent indentations in the surface.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: Three pumpkins in graduated sizes make a good first needle-felting set because the base shape is a simple sphere and the ribbing technique is a single repeated action. The

method wraps strong thread around a compacted ball at even intervals, then stabs along each wrapped line to push the merino inward, creating the characteristic pinched lobes of a pumpkin. The thread is cut away once all ribs are defined. The three pumpkins in this set are worked in orange merino (largest, 10 cm tall), deep orange merino (middle, 8 cm tall), and pale cream merino (smallest, 6 cm tall). Each has a short brown stem added last.

items.name: Brown merino roving (stems for all three)
Strong thread or thin string (for wrapping ribs)
Felting needle, 40-gauge (fine, for ribbing and detail)
Palm guard or folded kitchen sponge

content.text: Step 1: Build the three base balls

Start with the largest pumpkin. Take a large clump of orange merino and roll it into a ball in your palms. The pre-compaction ball for the largest pumpkin should be roughly 14 cm in diameter; it will compact to about 10 cm. Place the ball on the foam mat and stab with the 38-gauge needle, rotating the ball steadily to compact all sides evenly. Work for 8-10 minutes until the ball is firm and holds its round shape without any flat spots. It should measure approximately 10 cm in diameter. Repeat the process for the middle pumpkin (deep orange, aim for 8 cm diameter) and the smallest (pale cream, aim for 6 cm diameter).
Step 2: Flatten the top and bottom

A pumpkin is not a perfect sphere: the top and bottom are slightly flattened where the stem sits and where the pumpkin rests. Place each ball flat side down on the mat and stab straight down across the base to flatten it slightly, so the pumpkin sits stable without rolling. Then turn it over and stab down across the top to create a shallow depression at the stem point. The top depression should be about 1-2 cm across and about 5 mm deep. This is where the stem will be attached.
Step 3: Wrap and stab the ribs

Cut a length of strong thread, approximately 40 cm for the largest pumpkin. Tie one end around the ball so the thread sits in a line running from the top depression, over the equator, and down to the base. Tie it snugly but not so tight as to cut into the merino. Bring the thread back up and over the ball, crossing the top depression, to create a second line spaced evenly from the first. Continue wrapping until you have 5-6 evenly spaced lines running from top to base, like the sections of a pumpkin. Tie the thread off securely at the base.

Now stab along each thread line with the 40-gauge needle, placing the needle immediately beside the thread and stabbing straight in. Work along the full length of each line, stabbing every 3-4 mm. The goal is to push the merino inward along each line so the thread sinks into the surface and a ridge forms between the lines. After stabbing along all the thread lines, cut the thread away carefully with scissors. The indentations should hold as permanent ridges.

Once the thread is removed, use the 40-gauge needle to deepen any ribs that are not pronounced enough. Place the needle directly in the centre of each indentation line and stab along it again, working from top to base. Between each rib, the merino should bulge outward slightly to form a rounded lobe. If any lobe looks flat rather than rounded, pick up the ball and roll the flat section briefly in your palms to coax the fibre outward, then stab around the edges of the lobe to define it. Repeat the wrap-and-stab process for the middle and smallest pumpkins, adjusting the thread wrap count: 5 lines for the middle pumpkin, 4 lines for the smallest.
Step 5: Make and attach the stems

Roll small amounts of brown merino into short thick cylinders for the stems: approximately 2.5 cm long and 1 cm wide for the largest pumpkin, slightly shorter and narrower for the smaller two. Stab each stem on the mat until firm. Place each stem into the top depression of the matching pumpkin and stab through the base of the stem into the pumpkin body with the 38-gauge needle, using 10-12 firm strokes to lock the stem in place. The stem should stand upright and not wobble. Add a thin wisp of brown merino around the base of each stem and stab it in with the 40-gauge needle to smooth the join.

items.problem: The ribs are barely visible after removing the thread
items.cause: The thread was not pulled snug enough during wrapping, or the needle strokes along the thread lines were too shallow or too far from the thread
items.fix: Re-wrap the pumpkin with thread pulled firm against the surface and stab directly beside the thread, not 5 mm away from it. The needle should enter the merino right at the thread edge on each stroke.

items.problem: The ribs are uneven in spacing
items.cause: The thread was not distributed evenly around the ball before stabbing
items.fix: Use a soft measuring tape to divide the circumference into equal sections before wrapping. Mark the divisions lightly with a blunt pencil if needed, then place the thread at each mark.

items.problem: The stem falls over to one side
items.cause: The top depression was not deep or wide enough to seat the stem, or the stem was not stabbed firmly enough into the body
items.fix: Deepen the top depression with the 38-gauge needle before re-attaching the stem. Stab through the stem base into the body from at least three angles around the stem to lock it upright from multiple directions.

content.text: Brush each pumpkin gently with a dry soft-bristle brush to remove loose surface fibres. Do not machine-wash needle-felted pieces. The pumpkin set can be displayed outdoors briefly for decoration but should not be left in rain or direct sun for extended periods, as the merino will felt further if it becomes wet and agitated. Store in a sealed container away from wool moths between seasons.
